Recall that Sisko's final speech was specifically written, at Avery Brooks's insistence, to make it clear that he was coming back. A man with a family, even if he has been transformed into a godlike being existing out of space and time itself, is not free to just up and decide the universe has more important things for him than being a husband and father. Brooks was not about to let a Starfleet captain, and television's first black science fiction hero, be approvingly depicted doing that.

replies.text:
Gotta love their unequaled capacity for ticking all the right boxes and somehow still failing the test. 

The point of the original bridge crew was that we had managed to do away with profiling and construct a true meritocracy. Nobody cared Uhura was black because she could speak over a dozen languages and had an almost magical talent for decoding meaning from garbled transmissions. Nobody asked Sulu if he was great at math. We were PAST all that. The audience could see what greatness was possible if we just took whoever was best.

Now, the "progressive" show that's "not for us" has the black-coded character abandoned by both his dads, we can't say the word chains because it will hurt somebody's pwecious fee-fees, people eat com badges on a regular basis, and literally no one in what is essentially a _military academy_ displays even a crumb of competence or discipline. The writers are living down to ALL the lowest, cheapest stereotypes. No questions about the meaning of humanity, but let's have Klingons doing pop music. No means of properly answering such questions anyway, since the cleverness and panache of the dialog seems to be limited to stolen material that was better the first time around. And so on, and so forth.

Man, that got long. I've had this show recommended twice by my hyper-leftist, eat-the-slop, Trekkie brother-in-law so I guess I'm extra frustrated.

replies.author: @smakfu1375
replies.text:
Thing is, to an extent, so did old Trek, but then they would do something interesting: if they wanted to promote an idea that contradicted status-quo, or challenged contemporary views, or presented a theme, they’d illustrate both positions, or they’d structure a narrative progression. It was never just “shoved” down the audience’s throat. Doing that forced the writers to confront their own biases, use critical thought, come up with solutions to questions, and made for great story telling. 

Star Trek was always very progressive and, oddly, that’s part of its appeal to a lot of realist center-right people. I’ll openly admit that I love the idea of TNG’s space-socialist utopia. Of course, we also know they solved all the problems with socialism by inventing fictional infinite energy sources, a zero scarcity economy, and a highly automated technological future. These are all things that aren’t actually possible, at least not to our knowledge, but that’s the glorious promise: that technology and knowledge could create that gleaming future.

The problem is, for all the DC Fontana’s and Ron Moore’s involved with Trek, and for all the realist (and historically well educated nerdy) fans, it also attracted the (let’s be honest) not very bright useful idiots of the left. These are the unquestioning fools bereft of critical thinking skills. To them, old Trek was, at best, a show that confirmed their views, at worst, a dated “misogynist, racist, etc.” show. They didn’t understand the messaging, the interesting philosophical conflicts. They don’t get that Star Trek II was a statement about fascistic extremism and collectivist sacrifice, wrapped in a swashbuckling space adventure. All they see is toxic masculinity. From Roddenberry to Nick Meyer, almost everyone involved with Trek was a bleeding heart and had messaging injected in the shows and movies. But they knew they had to make it compelling: want to sell the “good of the many outweighs the good of the few, or the one” then you better wrap it in one hell of a story (and then, go back and make a second, underrated IMHO, movie that flips that idea on it’s side).

But fools like Kurtzman and his band of woke ideologues, are too lazy and too lacking in critical thinking skills to write a show that approaches a topic with care, integrity or intellectual curiosity. Nah, just be like a Pakled and stupidly beat people over-the-head with terribly written snarky dialogue and bludgeon the audience with “The Message”.

replies.author: @Brinx01
replies.text:
@ViolentMessiah666 The term "Mary Sue" originated in 1970s Star Trek fan fiction, coined by Paula Smith in her 1973 story A Trekkie's Tale, to use as an example of what not to submit to the fanzine she was editor of. She was sick of reading idealized self-insert characters who are beautiful, flawless, exceptionally talented, universally loved, and effortlessly save the day. Her story described a poorly-realized author-insert character, usually a young woman, who was too perfect, often romantically pursued by main characters (like Kirk or Spock), and lacked realistic flaws or basic human frailties. This character basically was introduced and became the focus of someone else's entire universe. Mary Sue was used specifically as a ridiculous example to illustrate what NOT to write if you wanted her to consider publishing your work. The term Gary Stu DID NOT predate Mary Sue. They came about at the same time.
Wesley Crusher was not a Gary Stu. He was a core character introduced in the first episode of TNG. You may not have liked him, but he is not a usurper of an established story. He's just an exceptionally smart character that some found annoying. He was not universally loved. The Star Trek universe didn't suddenly start to revolve around him. He was just the youngest member of the ensemble cast.

author: @onamission1848
text: I really hate when they say "we have to do a show like this or that so that someone can see their family represented on screen. I'm 51yo and in my entire life I have never seen my family represented on screen. Should I complain, should I cry, should I feel offended. No, because I don't watch movies or television to see a representation of myself. I watch to see something different. I'm not a specialist forces top comando, but I love watching movies about that. After all isn't that what movies are all about, giving us the chance to be someone else for  90 minutes?
